title: Modeling mixed boundary conditions in a Hilbert space with the complex variable boundary element method (CVBEM)
author: Johnson, Hromadka

steps_raw.title: Method details
steps_raw.instruction: The CVBEM has been developed by [1] for the solution of general problems involving Laplace or Poisson equations in multiple dimensions. Modeling begins with a simple closed contour of straight line segments. In a two dimensional complex plane, let ő© be bounded by a simple closed contour, őď, such that

Fig. 1 shows the global and local nodal numbering conventions. If one defines complex numbers ŌČji at each node zji, then degree k complex polynomials Njk(z) are uniquely defined on each boundary element őďj.

steps_raw.instruction: Along the boundary őď, or exterior to the problem domain union boundary, there are defined n nodal points. For development purposes, the n nodes are assumed defined on őď [8]. Note that the sum of the respective basis function terms is continuous on the boundary őď for all ő∂¬†‚ąą¬†őď. steps_raw.instruction: LettingRk(z)=12ŌÄiRk‚ąí1(z)+Nmk(z),then(17)ŌČňÜk(z)=Rk(z)‚ąí12ŌÄi‚ąĎPjk‚ąí1(z‚ąízj)ln(z‚ąízj).From (17), it is seen that ŌČňÜk(z) is continuous over ő© and has removable singularities at each boundary element endpoint (nodal coordinate zj, j¬†=¬†1, 2, 3, ‚Ä¶, m).That is Rk(z) and Pjk‚ąí1 are continuous complex polynomials, andlimz‚Üízj(z‚ąízj)ln(z‚ąízj)=0,i.e.ŌČňÜk(zj)=Rk(zj)Note that since ŌČňÜk(z) is analytic in ő© and ŌČňÜk(z)=ŌēňÜ(z)+iŌąňÜ(z) where ŌēňÜ(z) and ŌąňÜ(z) are two dimensional potential and stream functions which satisfy the Laplace equation exactly over ő©. By forcing the approximation values of ŌČňÜk(z) to be arbitrarily close (within some ŌĶ) to the boundary-condition values of ŌČ(z) on őď, then it is guaranteed by the maximum modulus theorem that the approximation of ŌČ(z) is bounded by |ŌČ(z)‚ąíŌČňÜ(z)|‚Č§ŌĶ, for all z¬†‚ąą¬†ő©.

steps_raw.title: Method details
steps_raw.instruction: Because the CVBEM results in a two-dimensional function which is an exact solution to the governing partial differential equation on ő©, convergence of ŌČňÜ(z) to ŌČ(z) is then achieved on ő©¬†‚ą™¬†őď by forcing convergence on őď. steps_raw.instruction: The global trial function is continuous. This characteristic separates the CVBEM from other approximations techniques. When solved, the CVBEM approximating integral becomes an approximating function of the form

steps_raw.instruction: Recall that ln¬†j includes the effect of the nodal point logarithmic branch cut rotations.

steps_raw.title: Analysis and numerical results
steps_raw.instruction: As an example of the complex variable boundary element method consider the twisting behavior of a homogeneous, isotropic shaft of an arbitrary, but uniform, cross section that is fixed at one end and subjected to a twisting couple at the other end. If the force and deformation behavior is of interest at some location somewhat removed from either end, then the stress and strain characteristics of the cross section as depicted in Fig. 7 are described by either of the following equations [3]:(38)‚ąā2Ōą(x,y)‚ąāx2+‚ąā2Ōą(x,y)‚ąāy2=0,(39)‚ąā2Ōē(x,y)‚ąāx2+‚ąā2Ōē(x,y)‚ąāy2=0.

steps_raw.title: Analysis and numerical results
steps_raw.instruction: The quantity Ōą(x, y) is the warping function of the cross-section whereas Ōē(x, y) is the conjugate of Ōą(x, y). If the warping function is known over the cross-section, then the out-of-plane warping displacement and the in-plane shear stresses can be calculated from the expressions

steps_raw.title: Analysis and numerical results
steps_raw.instruction: (40)ŌČ=őłŌą(x,y),ŌĄxz=őľőł‚ąāŌą(x,y)‚ąāx‚ąíy,ŌĄyz=őľőł‚ąāŌą(x,y)‚ąāy‚ąíx.

steps_raw.title: Analysis and numerical results
steps_raw.instruction: In the above expressions őł is the angle of the twist per unit length, őľ is the shear modulus, and x, y denote the coordinates of a point located from the center of twist. Furthermore, it should be noted that z represents a coordinate axis and should not be confused with the complex variable z¬†=¬†x¬†+¬†iy. If, on the other hand, the problem is posed in terms of the complementary function Ōē(x, y) then the shear stresses are determined from(41)ŌĄxz=őľőł‚ąāŌē(x,y)‚ąāy‚ąíy,ŌĄyz=őľőł‚ąí‚ąāŌē(x,y)‚ąāx+x.

steps_raw.title: Analysis and numerical results
steps_raw.instruction: While the form of Eqs. (38), (31) are identical, a solution strategy emerges depending on the manner in which the boundary conditions are specified. If the boundary condition of zero normal stress around the perimeter is posed, then a Neumann boundary condition, i.e. specified normal derivative, best describes the problem. In such a case the nonuniform torsion problem is best posed in terms of the warping function, Ōą(x, y). If on the other hand, the problem is best posed in terms of zero shear around the perimeter, then a Dirichlet boundary condition, i.e. specified functions, best describe the problem. In such a case the problem is best posed in terms of the complementary function, Ōē(x, y). While either solution method is well adapted for solid shafts, it is generally more convenient to operate directly with the warping function, Ōą(x, y), rather than its conjugate, Ōē(x, y), for hollow cross-sections.

steps_raw.title: Analysis and numerical results
steps_raw.instruction: The following example is used to analyze the CVBEM with established solutions [7] for shaft cross-sections of smooth and sharp corner profiles. Consider the torsion of a solid elliptical cross section with major axis a and minor axis b. The shear‚Äďstress-free boundary condition can be expressed in terms of the conjugate function Ōē(x, y) expressed on the boundary as(42)Ōē(x,y)=12(x2+y2)

steps_raw.title: Analysis and numerical results
steps_raw.instruction: The conjugate function Ōē(x, y) as well as the shear stresses can be shown to be(43)Ōē(x,y)=12(x2+y2)‚ąía2b2x2a2+y2b2‚ąí1(a2+b2)‚ąí1(44)ŌĄxz=‚ąíőľőł2ya2(a2+b2)(45)ŌĄyz=‚ąíőľőł2xb2(a2+b2)

steps_raw.title: Analysis and numerical results
steps_raw.instruction: Fig. 8 displays the approximate boundary for 6 node (a) collocation and (b) least squares models taken along a very small portion of the elliptical boundary. The deviation of Fig. 8a is due to error. Fig. 8b shows some error, but nearly as much. Fig. 9 shows the relative error for the 6-node model, respectively. The CVBEM relative error along the quarter elliptical section boundary is computed as [ŌēňÜ(x,y)‚ąíŌē(x,y)]/Ōē(x,y) where ŌēňÜ(x,y) is the CVBEM approximate solution and Ōē(x, y) is the exact solution. The quarter model of Fig. 7 was chosen to take advantage of the problem symmetry and to demonstrate the imposition of Ōē boundary conditions along the exterior curved edge and Ōą along the interior straight edge which is the side extending from the origin to the point (a, 0) and the line extending from the origin to the point (0, b) where a and b are 6.25 and 3.75 respectively. Table 1 summarizes the exact and computed warping function and shear‚Äďstress values at points in ő© using the collocation method. Table 2 demonstrates the exact and warping function calculation using least squares for the same number of basis functions, which are the nodes. The graphical depiction of the CVBEM in Fig. 10 uses computer programs Matlab, Mathematica, and MATLink to model the mixed boundary problem. Fig. 11 is a time analysis of the efficiency of collocation compared to least squares.
